claim: The recipes section (25 folios, 11,611 words, entropy 3.8586 bits â€” second highest and largest word count) encodes a list-structured text in which recurring syntactic templates produce predictable word-position bigrams, such that positional word-order entropy (entropy of word_n given word_{n-1} within a folio) is significantly lower in recipes than in the herbal or text-only sections.
evidence_for: Recipe and pharmaceutical texts in medieval Latin follow rigid syntactic templates (ingredient, quantity, preparation, application)
The recipes section has the highest word count (11,611), suggesting repetitive list structure
Entropy of 3.8586 is moderate â€” consistent with structured but not purely label-based text
evidence_against: Without knowing the cipher, word-level positional entropy is confounded by vocabulary domain effects
The herbal section (10,872 words) has similar entropy (3.8478), suggesting domain alone may explain the similarity
test: Compute word-level bigram conditional entropy H(w_n | w_{n-1}) for the recipes section vs. the herbal and text-only sections. Additionally, test whether the recipes section shows higher within-folio word repetition rates (fraction of words on a folio that appear more than once on that same folio).
test_metric: Word bigram conditional entropy per section; within-folio word repetition rate (fraction of repeated words per folio, averaged across folios in each section)
test_threshold: If recipes section word bigram conditional entropy is >0.3 bits lower than text-only section, template-driven structure is supported; if within-folio repetition rate in recipes exceeds herbal by >5 percentage points, list structure is confirmed

tests_run.test: compute word-level bigram conditional entropy h(w_n | w_{n-1}) for the recipes section vs. the herba
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: Confirming list/template structure in the recipes section would allow researchers to use positional slot analysis (slot 1 = ingredient name, slot 2 = quantity, etc.) as a structural scaffold for decipherment, independently of knowing the cipher
